keyPoints: Longitudinal tracking of time use, perceived meaning, and engagement in long-term projects across sector-level automation exposure.
Substitution vs. complementarity pathways conditioned by task content and autonomy, with meaning and welfare outcomes.
CMM-based meaning measures paired with high-granularity time-use diaries for robust meaning-welfare estimation.
Mediation and moderation analyses to test time reallocation as the conduit between automation exposure and welfare/meaning.
Multi-level design: individuals nested in sectors/regions with waves across 5–10 years to capture diffusion dynamics.
Policy-relevant conditionals: retraining, task redesign, and work design features that sustain autonomy and long-horizon work.
Cross-country harmonization and integration of automation exposure indices (AIOE/C-AIOE/ESCO-DCI/GENOE) for comparability.
